title: Starter Character
description: A small example schema bundled with Bot Manager Kit. It defines a basic character — name, personality, likes, goals — and is meant as a starting point for your own schemas. Copy this folder under BotSchemas/ to a new name, edit `properties` to taste, and your version will show up in the schema picker on next launch.
$comment: Schema author notes (these `$comment` fields are ignored by validators and the UI; they exist purely as guidance for the next person who edits this file). Required-by-the-kit fields: `id` and `lastModified` are stamped/managed automatically and most schemas will keep them as-is. `additionalProperties: false` at the bottom means typos and unknown fields are rejected on save — flip it to `true` if you want a more permissive schema during early experimentation.

id.description: Unique identifier. Generated by the kit when a bot is created — don't edit by hand.
id.$comment: Required by the kit for stable bot referencing. Keep this field on any schema you write.

name.description: Display name shown on the Library card and the editor title bar.

lastModified.description: Last edit timestamp. Stamped automatically by the kit on save.
lastModified.$comment: Required by the kit for sorting bots by recency. Keep this field on any schema you write.

tagline.description: One-line hook that captures the character at a glance.
examples: The reluctant heir to a dying mountain kingdom.
A bartender who remembers every face but trusts none.
tagline.$comment: Demonstrates a short-text field with a length cap. Adjust maxLength or remove it as you like.

sex.description: How the character identifies. Free-form by default so the schema stays flexible.

age.$comment: Demonstrates a numeric field. Tighten the range with `maximum`, or use `type: "number"` for fractional values.

personality.description: Personality, temperament, and demeanor. A paragraph or two of prose works well.
examples: Outwardly cheerful and quick with a joke, but slow to trust. Defaults to deflection when conversations get serious.

backstory.description: Character history, formative experiences, and motivations. Prose.
backstory.$comment: If you later want to split backstory into structured sub-fields (birthplace, family, formative_event, etc.) replace this string with `type: "object"` and define `properties`. The kit will render a sub-panel.

goals.description: What the character wants — short-term, long-term, or both. Prose.
goals.$comment: Could also be modelled as an array of structured goals; see the `likes` field below for the array pattern.

likes.description: Things the character is drawn to or enjoys.

dislikes.description: Things the character avoids, resents, or is unsettled by.
dislikes.$comment: Mirror of `likes` — both demonstrate the `array of strings` pattern. To collect structured items instead (each with its own sub-fields), change `items` from `{"type": "string"}` to `{"$ref": "#/definitions/SomeBlock"}` and define the block under `definitions` at the bottom of the file.

images.description: Filenames of images attached to this character. Managed by the kit's Images view — don't edit by hand.
images.$comment: Remove this field (and `profileImage` below) if your schema doesn't need images. They're kept here because the kit's Library page uses `profileImage` for card thumbnails.

profileImage.description: Filename (drawn from `images` above) used as the Library card thumbnail.

$comment: Extending this schema: add new fields under `properties` and, if any are required, list their names in `required`. The kit's editor tree builds itself from the schema, so a new field shows up in the editor on next launch — no code changes needed. Common extensions: an `appearance` object with height/build/hair sub-fields; an array of structured `relationships` (name/role/notes); a `voice` field for speech style; a `quirks` array for distinctive mannerisms. Look at the GrokBot schema in bot-manager-desktop for a heavier worked example.
